editorNote: A curated view of 10 stories shaping today's Hacker News conversation.

stories.headline: China’s open-weights AI strategy is winning
stories.summary: Link: China delivers a one-two punch to America’s AI dominance, by Robert Hart in The Verge AI models, as a product in themselves, have very little moat beyond what amounts to brand loyalty and superficial switching costs.  Instead, the moat is in the enterprise services that sit around them: the deals and contracts, connectivity with enterprise systems, and quality of life features in an enterprise context.
stories.whyItMatters: The Hacker News community gave this story 1108 points.

stories.headline: Hacker wipes Romania's land registry database

stories.headline: Who's afraid of Chinese models?
stories.summary: Who’s Afraid of Chinese Models?  Monday, July 20, 2026 Listen to Podcast Listen to this post : Log in to listen There’s a story I tell about my first day in STRT-431 at Kellogg School of Management, the introductory class that every first-year MBA was required to take; I leafed through the readings and case studies and was dismayed that there weren’t any tech companies on the docket.

stories.headline: Jelly UI: Soft-body physics for native HTML form controls
stories.summary: It's okay to be a little jelly Jelly UI is a dependency-free Web Components library for soft, tactile product interfaces.  Real form controls meet soft-body physics, with dark mode, right-to-left support and WCAG AA color tokens built in.

stories.headline: How we measured AI writing across arXiv, and where the measurement breaks
stories.summary: How we measured AI writing across arXiv, and where the measurement breaks We scored the full text of 12,750 arXiv papers and found that about a third of new ones read as machine-written.  Here is the method, the results, and an honest account of the limitations.
